title: chi^O(H) well-definedness as minimum (not infimum) not verified

comments.author: critic-agent
comments.body:
## Gap: Characteristic function well-definedness

The solution defines the characteristic function as
$$\chi^\mathcal{O}(H) := \min\{K \mid K \to H\} = \bigcap_{K \to H} K.$$
For the equality to hold — i.e., for the intersection to be the *minimum* rather than merely the greatest lower bound — one must show that $\bigcap_{K \to H} K$ is itself admissible (i.e., satisfies $\bigcap_{K \to H} K \to H$).

This follows from the axioms of transfer systems but is not proved:
- If $K_1 \to H$ and $K_2 \to H$, apply axiom 3 (closure under restriction) with $J = K_2 \subseteq K = H$: $K_1 \cap K_2 \to K_2$. By transitivity of the partial order ($K_2 \to H$), $K_1 \cap K_2 \to H$.
- By induction (using finiteness of $G$), any finite intersection of admissible subgroups is admissible.
- Hence $\chi^\mathcal{O}(H) = \bigcap_{K \to H} K$ is admissible, i.e., the minimum exists.

Without this verification, the characteristic function is not well-defined as a *minimum*, only as an infimum in the subgroup lattice. The rest of the proof relies on $\chi^\mathcal{O}(H)$ being admissible (it is used as an admissible subgroup in Lemma 2 lower bound: "choose admissible $T$ with ... namely $T = k G/\chi^\mathcal{O}(G)$").

This is a minor gap that is straightforward to close with a one-sentence lemma, but it is logically required.

comments.author: solver-agent
comments.body:
## Fix: well-definedness of $\chi^{\mathcal O}(H)$ as a genuine minimum

I replaced the one-sentence justification with an explicit lemma (Lemma `lem:chi-min`, "$\chi^{\mathcal O}$ is well defined as a minimum") and proof, exactly along the lines the critic suggested.

**What is now proved.** For $\mathcal A_H := \{K \mid K \to H\}$:
1. **Contains $H$:** a transfer system is a partial order, hence reflexive, so $H \to H$.
2. **Pairwise intersection closure:** if $K_1 \to H$ and $K_2 \to H$, then by axiom (1) $K_2 \subseteq H$, so axiom (3) applied to $K_1 \to H$ with $J = K_2 \subseteq H$ gives $K_1 \cap K_2 \to K_2$; transitivity of $\to$ with $K_2 \to H$ then yields $K_1 \cap K_2 \to H$.
3. **Finite closure:** by induction on the number of factors, any finite intersection of members of $\mathcal A_H$ is in $\mathcal A_H$.
4. **Minimum attained:** since $G$ is finite, $\operatorname{Sub}(G)$ is finite, so $\mathcal A_H$ is finite; hence $K_\ast := \bigcap_{K\to H} K$ is a *finite* intersection of members of $\mathcal A_H$, so $K_\ast \in \mathcal A_H$, i.e. $K_\ast \to H$. Being contained in every member, it is the least element. Thus $\min\{K \mid K \to H\}$ exists and equals $\bigcap_{K\to H} K$.

I also added a follow-up sentence noting that $\chi^{\mathcal O}(H) \to H$ (admissibility into $H$) is precisely the property used downstream (e.g. Lemma `lem:index`(1), where $H/\chi^{\mathcal O}(H)$ must be admissible, and the Lemma 2 lower bound using $T = kG/\chi^{\mathcal O}(G)$).

**Numerical sanity check.** I exhaustively enumerated all 19 transfer systems on the Klein four-group $V_4$ (the smallest group with a non-trivial, non-linear subgroup lattice) and verified that for every transfer system and every subgroup $H$, the intersection $\bigcap_{K\to H} K$ is itself admissible into $H$ — i.e. the minimum is always attained. Zero failures.

The gap is fully closed; the relevant finiteness hypothesis ($G$ finite, already a standing assumption) is used explicitly to pass from pairwise/finite closure to "the intersection over the whole set is attained."
